name: Tanngrisnir
description: Tanngrisnir, meaning 'teeth-gnasher,' is one of the two goats that pull the chariot of the thunder god Thor in Norse mythology. Along with his brother Tanngnjóstr ('teeth-grinder'), Tanngrisnir is not merely an animal companion but a crucial element of Thor's power and travel. These goats possess the remarkable ability to be slaughtered, cooked, and eaten by Thor and his companions, only to be resurrected the next day, bone for bone. This regenerative power is vital for Thor's long journeys across Midgard, providing a reliable source of sustenance. However, the resurrection is contingent upon all the bones remaining intact. In one famous tale, a farmer's son, Þjálfi, breaks one of Tanngrisnir's leg bones to suck out the marrow. This act of disrespect results in the goat being lame upon resurrection, and Thor takes Þjálfi and his sister Röskva as his servants in compensation. This story highlights the importance of respecting the divine and the consequences of disrespecting even seemingly 'lesser' beings associated with the gods. Tanngrisnir represents not only Thor's power and ability to travel but also themes of sacrifice, renewal, and the delicate balance between mortals and the divine.
subtitle: Thor's regenerative chariot-pulling goat

keyMyths.title: Thor's Journey to Utgard
keyMyths.description: Thor travels to Utgard with Loki, Þjálfi, and Röskva, relying on Tanngrisnir and Tanngnjóstr for transport and sustenance. The goats are slaughtered and eaten each night, then resurrected the following morning.

keyMyths.title: Þjálfi Breaks the Bone
keyMyths.description: During the journey to Utgard, Þjálfi breaks one of Tanngrisnir's leg bones to suck out the marrow, causing the goat to be lame upon resurrection. Thor punishes Þjálfi by taking him into service.

keyMyths.title: Resurrection Ritual
keyMyths.description: The resurrection of Tanngrisnir and Tanngnjóstr requires all bones to be intact and placed properly. This highlights the importance of respect and proper conduct when dealing with divine beings and their associated creatures.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: A primary source for Norse mythology, including tales of Thor and his goats.
sources.text: A primary source for Norse mythology, including tales of Thor and his goats.

sources.author: Hilda Ellis Davidson

sources.description: A comprehensive overview of Norse mythology and religion.
